Some of the basidiomycetes forcibly discharge their spores from the basidia, which remain after spore discharge. The Central Intelligence Agency on Tuesday became the first major national security agency to offer so-called buyouts to its entire workforce, a CIA spokesperson and two other sources familiar with the offer said, part of President Donald Trump’s broad effort to shrink the federal government and shape it to his agenda.

definitions: The audible friction of voiced consonants.
A whisper.
A continuous humming noise, as of bees; a confused murmur, as of general conversation in low tones.
(informal) Major topic of conversation; widespread rumor; information spread behind the scenes.
Synonym of fizz-buzz (“ counting game ”).
(informal) A telephone call or e-mail.
(informal) A rush or feeling of energy or excitement; a feeling of slight intoxication.
examples: Still feeling the buzz from the coffee, he pushed through the last of the homework.
Now, don't waste time, give us a buzz -- quick! 825-4703 or 265-7881.
Allen Gregory DeLongpre: Who's he?
I wasn't performing with Pimp and Smoove that much no more, but I had a solo deal with Ruthless Rap and a brand new mixtape that was creating a big buzz.
Patrick: He's only the most popular kid in school.

definitions: (colloquial) To show a high level of activity and haste, energization or excitement, to be busy as a bee in one’s actions but perhaps mentally charged.
To talk to incessantly or confidentially in a low humming voice.
To cut (the hair) in a close-cropped military style, or buzzcut.
(transitive, aviation, also figuratively) To fly at high speed and at a very low altitude over (an area), as to make a surprise pass.
To communicate with (a person) by means of a buzzer.
To whisper; to communicate, as tales, in an undertone; to spread, as a report, by whispers or secretly.
To make a low, continuous, humming or sibilant sound, like that made by bees with their wings.
To throw swiftly.
examples: A fly was buzzing past me.
I will buzz abroad such prophecies / That Edward shall be fearful of his life.
However these disturbers of our peace / Buzz in the people's ears.
But I tell you this has set all London buzzing. The old man is where he likes to be, with a pin-point limelight right on his hairy old head.
Ah! the singing, fatal arrow, / Like a wasp it buzzed, and stung him [a roebuck]!

definitions: (US, military slang, World War I) A fighter plane.
(US, military slang, 20th century) The insignia of a colonel, or a petty officer within the navy.
Any of several Old World birds of prey of the genus Buteo with broad wings and a broad tail.
(US, military slang, 1860s–1920s) A military discharge (due to the military discharge certificate).
(colloquial, derogatory, slang, often preceded by "old", the "old buzzard") In North America, a curmudgeonly or cantankerous man; an old person; a mean, greedy person.
(euphemistic) A bastard.
(Canada, US) Any scavenging bird, such as the American black vulture (Coragyps atratus) or the turkey vulture (Cathartes aura).
(golf) Synonym of double bogey.
examples: It is common, to a proverb, to call one who can not be taught, or who continues obstinately ignorant, a buzzard.
An old man’s shadow is better than a young buzzard ’s sword.
Perhaps the crusty old buzzard loved his only child more than anyone had given him credit for all these years — maybe even more than he himself had realized.

definitions: plural of buzzard
One who, or that which, buzzes; an insect that buzzes.
(cricket, slang) A run scored from an overthrow.
A device that makes a buzzing sound.
(US slang) A police badge.
examples: And wants not buzzers to infect his ear / With pestilent speeches of his father's death.
If you think you know the answer to the question, hit the buzzer as fast as you can.
I flipped my wallet open on her desk and let her look at the buzzer pinned to the flap.
The pickpocket was a Buzzer. The man who booked a room at a hotel, and went round the rooms of other guests, was a Snoozer.
Her brother is in secret come from France, Feeds on this wonder, keeps himself in clouds And wants not buzzers to infect his ear With pestilent speeches of his father's death.

definitions: The sound produced by something that buzzes.
gerund of buzz
examples: I can hear buzzing coming from the television.

definitions: present participle and gerund of buzz
(UK, slang) Very happy; full of joyful excitement.
examples: “Nice one? It's amazing! There's loads like that,” he tells me. “Everyone is raving about you.” I'm totally buzzing. It's a shame Mum and Granddad aren't here.

definitions: (sometimes derogatory) A word drawn from, or imitative of, technical jargon, used more to impress others than to convey meaning.
examples: Their salespeople know all the right buzzwords, but they can't really help you solve your problems.

definitions: Out of the way, off to one side.
Subsidiary, incidental.
examples: by catch; a by issue
a by path; a by room

definitions: Along a path which runs past someone or something.
In the vicinity, near.
(uncommon except in set phrases) Beyond or past a certain point.
(uncommon outside the phrase ' put by ') Aside, away.
To or at a place, as a residence or place of business.
examples: We're right near the lifeguard station. Come by before you leave.
I'll stop by on my way home from work.
I watched the parade as it passed by.
I'll somehow manage to get by.
There was a shepherd close by.

definitions: Not later than (the given time); not later than the end of (the given time interval).
Indicates the person or thing that does or causes something: Through the action or presence of.
Indicates a referenced source: According to.
Indicates the amount of change, difference or discrepancy
Indicates the creator of a work: Existing through the authorship etc. of.
From one side of something to the other, passing close by; past.
Indicates a means of achieving something: Involving/using the means of.
Used to separate dimensions when describing the size of something.
examples: Table 1 shows details of our employees broken down by sex and age.
The matter was decided by the chairman.
By my reckoning, we should be nearly there.
By Chabad, it's different.
By all that is holy, I'll put an end to this.

definitions: Any fish (or other creatures) that are not targeted as a catch but are unintentionally caught, and often discarded back into the sea.
Any person, animal, or thing, captured unintentionally on camera or film.
examples: Some offer him bags of bycatch —fish too small or net-mangled to sell, rays and seahorses few will want, and so on.

definitions: To catch unintentionally while fishing for something else.
(cricket) An extra scored when the batsmen take runs after the ball has passed the striker without hitting either the bat or the batsman.
(Scotland) An unspecified way or place.
(card games) A pass.
Eye dialect spelling of boy.

The position of a person or team in a tournament or competition who draws no opponent in a particular round so advances to the next round unopposed, or is awarded points for a win in a league table; also the phantom opponent of such a person or team.
examples: No word of a new house-keeper down bye, Wull?

definitions: Out of the way; remote.
Secondary; supplementary.
examples: At length having gained a very bye Alley, where he thought he might enter into a Conference unnoticed by any who knew him.

definitions: (Canada, New Zealand, often UK) Alternative form of by-election.
An event that happened in the past.
An object from the past; a relic, antique, etc.
examples: And now just as the Victorians collected the minute objects with which to fill these houses, so today grown-ups are paying large sums for these bygones, while the children are given more modern houses to play with.

definitions: Having been or happened in the distant past.
examples: Near by he could see the thicket of raspberry canes, growing tall and close like a tropical jungle, in whose shadow he had played with the Boy on bygone mornings.

definitions: A rule made by a local authority to regulate its own affairs.
A law or rule governing the internal affairs of an organization (e.g., corporation or business).
A local custom or law of a settlement or district.
(sports) A touchline.
(journalism) A line at the head of a newspaper or magazine article carrying the writer 's name.
examples: Paste Magazine opted not to put a byline on its harsh review of Swift’s album, citing safety concerns for the writer.

definitions: (journalism, transitive) To provide (an article) with a byline.
examples: Mitchell, Lange and Brus (2013) find that in a top international relations Journal [sic] —ISQ—83 percent of men's citation are to research bylined by just men, while 57 percent of women's citations are to articles bylined by just men (table 2).

definitions: (Germanic paganism) A ritual title for a god or goddess used in Heathenry.
(medicine) An alternative passage created to divert a bodily fluid around a damaged organ; the surgical procedure to construct such a bypass.
The act of going past or around.
An electrical shunt.
A road that passes around something, such as a residential area or business district.
A section of pipe that conducts a fluid around some other fixture.
examples: Five of the 16 patients required simultaneous FF bypass and iliaco-femoral bypass; 2, required simultaneous FF bypass and iliac thrombo-endoarterectomy (Table II).

definitions: To ignore the usual channels or procedures.
To avoid an obstacle etc, by constructing or using a bypass.
examples: Datacasting bypasses the wired, terrestrial Internet and is a cheaper way to distribute software than pressing and mailing CDs.

definitions: past
examples: The Hospital […] for these two bypast years has contained not less than 150 beds, generally occupied by a full amount of patients.

definitions: An unfrequented path; an indirect route; a byway.
examples: During these days, the boy rode Sir Mortimer abroad in many directions until he knew every bypath within a radius of fifty miles of Torn. Sometimes the old man accompanied him, but more often he rode alone.

definitions: (theater) Any action, carried out onstage during a performance, apart from the main action.
Any action, by supporting actor.
examples: The father-son byplay is occasionally overdone and tedious, but there is a genuine warmth in both the Oland-Luke and the Toler-Sen Yung teamings.

definitions: (chiefly British) A barn, especially one used for keeping cattle in.
examples: It was here in the kitchen, in the passage, In the mews in the harn in the byre in the market-place [...]

definitions: A road less frequented than a highway; a byway.
(mycology) The stipe or stem of some fungi which are particularly thin and thread-like.
Sea silk manufactured from these filaments.
The long fine silky filaments excreted by several mollusks (particularly Pinna nobilis) by which they attach themselves to the sea bed, and from which sea silk is manufactured.
A person who, although present at some event, does not take part in it; an observer, witness, or spectator.
examples: If he wins, he will be at liberty, perhaps, to give vent to a laugh, or to pass a remark on the circumstance to a bystander, or to stake again, or to double his stake; but, even this he must do solely out of curiosity, and for the pleasure of watching the play of chances and of calculations, and not because of any vulgar desire to win.

definitions: (computing) A short sequence of bits (binary digits) that can be operated on as a unit by a computer; the smallest usable machine word.
(computing, most commonly) A unit of computing storage equal to eight bits, which can represent any of 256 distinct values.
examples: The word “hello” fits into five bytes of ASCII code.

definitions: A road that is not frequently travelled.
(by extension) a little-known or arcane field of study or detail.
A road or track not following a main route; a secondary or minor road or path.
examples: Near-synonyms: back road, back street, back lane
Although some of its individuality has been lost, this railway byway in North Essex still retains its charm, and is well worthy of exploration by railway enthusiasts.
Though his account of written communication over the past 5,000 years necessarily has a powerful forward momentum, his diversions down the fascinating byways of the subject are irresistible...

definitions: Someone or something that stands as an example (i.e. metonymically) for something else, by having some of that something's characteristic traits.
A nickname or epithet.
A proverb or proverbial expression, common saying; a frequently used word or phrase.
An object of notoriety or contempt, scorn or derision.
A characteristic word or expression; a word or phrase associated with a person or group.
examples: He hath made me also a byword of the people […]

Illustrious unfortunates attract a wider sympathy, not because their griefs are more intense, but because, being set on lofty pedestals, they the better serve mankind as instances and bywords of calamity.

definitions: Alternative letter-case form of Byzantine (figurative senses only).
examples: The result is a byzantine system of government directed by 160 ministers, a structure that absorbs 50 percent of Bosnia’s gross domestic product of $15 billion, according to the World Bank.
